planting.note: Set out from containers in spring or fall in full sun for best color.

directions: Plant this dense, upright dwarf at pot depth and give it bright light to intensify its coloring. It keeps a compact, refined shape with minimal pruning. Vivid red new foliage contrasts against deep green mature leaves, and the whole plant reddens further through cold weather.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun to partial shade with good drainage.

directions: Set this dwarf at the same depth as its nursery pot and water regularly the first year. Its tidy mound needs little pruning; simply trim stray growth to shape. Repeated flushes of soft pink new foliage give it an ever-changing look, turning brighter red in cool weather.

planting.note: Plant from containers in spring or fall in sun to light shade.

directions: Plant this bright, rounded selection where its chartreuse foliage can light up a shady corner. It maintains a naturally full shape and rarely needs pruning. New leaves emerge lime-green and mature to a soft yellow-green, staying colorful without the winter reddening of many kin.

planting.note: Set out in spring or fall; its narrow habit suits tight spaces.

directions: Plant this upright, columnar cultivar at pot depth for a vertical accent in narrow beds. Thin the oldest canes occasionally to keep growth dense from the base. Fiery red-orange spring foliage matures to green and returns to bright red in the cool of fall and winter.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in a sheltered, well-drained spot.

directions: Set this lacy-leaved form where its fine, filamentous texture can be appreciated up close. Its soft, feathery mound needs little pruning beyond thinning old canes. The delicate green leaflets flush reddish-bronze in autumn, giving a soft, almost fern-like winter appearance.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; group several for reliable fruit set.

directions: Plant this fruiting form at crown level and thin a third of the oldest canes each late winter for renewal. Full sun encourages both flowering and berry set. White summer flower panicles give way to unusual creamy-white to pale-yellow berries instead of the typical red.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in partial shade to protect the variegation.

directions: Site this variegated selection out of the hottest afternoon sun to keep its cream-and-green markings crisp. Its upright habit stays tidy with only light thinning of old canes. Leaves are heavily splashed white, cream, and pink, taking on rosier tones as temperatures drop.

planting.note: Plant potted dwarfs in spring or fall in full sun for strongest color.

directions: Set this compact, mounding dwarf at pot depth and give it full sun to maximize its winter tones. It holds a dense, rounded form with essentially no pruning. Bright green summer foliage transforms to vivid scarlet and orange-red through fall and winter cold.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; notable for heavy fruit set without a pollinator.

directions: Plant this upright, self-fruitful selection at crown level and renew old canes yearly for vigor. Unlike some cultivars it sets abundant berries even planted alone. Large white flower clusters in summer give way to dense, showy crops of red berries that hold well into winter.

planting.note: Set out tall-growing plants in spring or fall as a screen or backdrop.

directions: Plant this vigorous, upright cultivar where its greater height can anchor the back of a border. Thin the oldest canes each late winter to keep foliage from bunching at the top. It produces full summer flower panicles and red berries, with foliage reddening in winter cold.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil in sun to light shade.

directions: Set this graceful, finer-textured selection at crown level and mulch to hold moisture. Its softer, more delicate foliage benefits from light thinning of old canes. Lacy green leaves take on warm reddish tones in fall and winter, giving a refined, airy look.

planting.note: Plant tall specimens in spring or fall for height and structure.

directions: Plant this large, robust cultivar where its bold height and coarse foliage make a statement. Renew a third of the oldest canes annually to keep the clump dense from the ground up. Big white flower clusters yield heavy red berry sprays, and leaves flush red in winter.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun for the most intense red color.

directions: Set this compact, colorful selection at pot depth and give it full sun to fuel its fiery tones. Its dense, mounding habit stays neat with minimal pruning. Foliage flames bright red in cold weather and softens to green through the warm season, offering strong year-round interest.

planting.note: Plant potted dwarfs in spring or fall in sun to partial shade.

directions: Set this stiff, low dwarf at the same depth it grew in its container and water to establish. Its cushion-like form needs virtually no pruning. The thick, cupped leaves turn a deep reddish-purple in winter, holding rich color best in bright sun.

planting.note: Set container plants out in spring or early autumn into acidic, moist soil.

directions: Plant in humus-rich, acidic, well-drained soil at the same depth it grew in the pot and mulch to keep the shallow roots cool. Shelter from cold drying winds and water through dry spells the first two seasons. Drooping clusters of urn-shaped flowers open in early spring above bronzy new leaves.

planting.note: This cold-hardy North American native settles in best planted in spring.

directions: Give this mounded native shrub acidic, well-drained soil and light shade, keeping the root zone mulched and evenly moist. It tolerates more cold and sun than Japanese pieris but resents soggy ground. Upright rather than drooping flower spikes carry white bells in early spring.

planting.note: Plant this larger, tender-tipped species in spring in a sheltered spot.

directions: Site this vigorous shrub in acidic, moist but free-draining soil with wind protection, since late frosts can scorch the vivid red young growth. Mulch yearly and avoid disturbing the surface roots. Large panicles of white flowers follow the flush of scarlet new foliage in spring.

planting.note: Plant this southeastern native in spring in acidic, boggy woodland soil.

directions: Grow this unusual native in consistently moist, acidic soil at a woodland or bog edge where it can scramble up nearby trunks. Keep it well watered, as it naturally grows in wet flatwoods and swamp margins. Small white urn-shaped flowers appear along the stems in late winter to early spring.

planting.note: Plant this popular hybrid in spring in a spot sheltered from late frost.

directions: Plant in acidic, moisture-retentive soil in dappled shade, mulching over the shallow roots and shielding the tender young growth from cold wind. The new leaves emerge brilliant red, then age through pink and cream to green. Long white flower trusses open in spring as the fiery foliage develops.

planting.note: Plant this brilliant red-flushed selection in spring with frost shelter.

directions: Grow in rich, acidic, well-drained soil in light shade sheltered from spring frosts that can damage the flaming new foliage. Keep the roots cool with mulch and steady moisture. Sealing-wax-red young leaves are joined by drooping sprays of white flowers in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Plant this hardy hybrid in spring or early fall in acidic soil.

directions: This cross between mountain and Japanese pieris takes cold well; plant it in acidic, well-drained soil with a cool mulched root run. It holds dark reddish flower buds through winter for early spring interest. Upright and spreading trusses of white bells open in early spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in acidic soil sheltered from harsh wind.

directions: Plant in moist, acidic, well-drained soil in part shade and mulch to protect the surface roots. Prized for the glossy fire-red flush of its new leaves each spring. Drooping clusters of white flowers open in early spring before the leaves color up.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in acidic, humus-rich soil.

directions: Set into acidic, moisture-retentive soil in light shade and keep the root zone cool with mulch. Deep maroon flower buds hold through winter, opening to long, drooping sprays of dark rose-red bells. Flowers appear in early spring and last for several weeks.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in cool, acidic, well-drained soil.

directions: Grow in acidic, humus-rich soil in part shade with an even supply of moisture. Mulch yearly and shelter from drying winds. Soft pink urn-shaped flowers hang in graceful chains in early to mid spring.

planting.note: Plant this variegated form in spring in a lightly shaded, sheltered spot.

directions: Plant in acidic, well-drained soil in dappled light, which keeps the leaf margins brightest. The new growth flushes red before settling to green leaves edged in silvery white. White flowers dangle in spring, complementing the crisp variegation.

planting.note: Plant in spring in part shade to protect the pale variegation from scorch.

directions: Give this slow-growing form acidic, moist, well-drained soil in light shade so the cream leaf edges do not burn. Mulch to keep the roots cool and steadily moist. White bell flowers hang in spring above foliage margined in creamy white.

planting.note: Plant this compact selection in spring in acidic, well-drained soil.

directions: Ideal for small gardens and containers, this dense, low mound wants acidic soil and light shade. Mulch to protect the shallow roots and water in dry spells. Upright spikes of pure white flowers stand above the foliage in early spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in acidic, humus-rich soil in part shade.

directions: Grow in cool, acidic, well-drained soil with a mulched root zone and shelter from wind. The wine-red new leaves are a standout feature through the growing season. Cascading rose-pink flowers open in early spring, sometimes with a light rebloom in autumn.

planting.note: Plant this dwarf form in spring in acidic, well-drained soil.

directions: A tidy dwarf for foundations and containers, plant it in acidic, moisture-retentive soil in part shade. Mulch to keep roots cool and avoid letting it dry out. Dense clusters of creamy white bells cover the compact plant in mid spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in acidic soil; hardy and reliable.

directions: Plant in acidic, well-drained soil in light shade and mulch over the shallow roots. Deep red-purple flower buds provide winter color before opening. The buds open to pale pink-blushed white flowers in early spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in acidic, moist, well-drained soil.

directions: Set into humus-rich, acidic soil in part shade and keep the roots cool and evenly moist. This selection is noted for an exceptionally heavy flush of bloom. Long, drooping chains of pure white flowers smother the plant in early to mid spring.

planting.note: Plant this compact white form in spring in acidic soil.

directions: A compact, free-flowering form for small spaces and pots, grown in acidic, well-drained soil in light shade. Mulch and water to keep the shallow roots from drying. Large, pure white bells appear even on young plants in early spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in acidic, sheltered soil to protect the tender new growth.

directions: Grow in acidic, moist, well-drained soil in part shade with shelter from wind. This form is grown mainly for its striking salmon-pink young foliage that fades to cream then green. Modest sprays of white flowers appear in spring.

planting.note: Plant this dwarf variegated form in spring in acidic soil in light shade.

directions: A slow, rounded dwarf for edging and containers, set it in acidic, well-drained soil in dappled shade. The small leaves are edged silver-white and flush pink in spring. It flowers only sparingly, so it is grown chiefly for its neat variegated foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in acidic, well-drained soil in part shade.

directions: Plant in cool, acidic, moist soil in light shade and mulch over the surface roots. This early bloomer often opens in mild spells at the tail of winter. Its white flowers are tipped and flushed deep pink at the mouth.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in acidic, humus-rich soil.

directions: Grow in acidic, well-drained soil in part shade with a cool, mulched root zone. Bronze-red new growth contrasts with heavy sprays of clean white flowers. Blooms open in early spring and hold well over the foliage.

planting.note: Plant this dwarf form in spring in acidic, well-drained soil.

directions: A dense, mounding dwarf well suited to rock gardens and containers, plant it in acidic, moist soil in light shade. Keep the shallow roots cool with mulch and steady moisture. Small white flowers cover the tidy plant in early spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in acidic, well-drained soil in part shade.

directions: Plant in acidic, humus-rich soil in light shade and mulch to keep the roots cool. Coppery new leaves set off the heavy display of bloom. Broad clusters of white flowers open in early to mid spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in acidic, moist, well-drained soil.

directions: Grow in cool, acidic soil in part shade and mulch over the shallow root system. Free-flowering, with an abundance of soft pink bells. The drooping flower chains open in early to mid spring.

planting.note: Plant this late-flowering, hardy form in spring or fall in acidic soil.

directions: Plant in acidic, well-drained soil in part shade with a mulched, evenly moist root zone. Vivid red new growth is a highlight, and it flowers later than most, helping it dodge frost. White flower sprays open in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Plant this dense form in spring or fall in acidic, well-drained soil.

directions: Set into acidic, moist soil in light shade and mulch to protect the shallow roots. Slower and denser than the species, it makes a neat rounded shrub. Profuse white flowers cover the plant in early spring.

planting.note: Plant this variegated dwarf in spring in acidic soil in light shade.

directions: A compact variegated form for pots and small beds, plant in acidic, well-drained soil in dappled light. The leaves are edged in white and flush pink-red when young. White flowers hang in short chains in early spring.

planting.note: Plant this slow, compact form in spring in acidic, well-drained soil.

directions: A slow-growing, spreading form for the front of a bed, grown in acidic, moist soil in part shade. Mulch to keep the shallow roots cool and moist. Creamy white bell flowers hang thickly in early spring.

planting.note: Plant this dwarf white form in spring in acidic, well-drained soil.

directions: A dense dwarf for small gardens and containers, set it in acidic, moist soil in light shade. Keep the root zone mulched and evenly watered. Upright spikes of white flowers stand above narrow foliage in early spring.

planting.note: Plant this compact form in spring in acidic, well-drained soil.

directions: A neat, low form good for foundations and pots, plant in acidic, moist soil in part shade. Mulch to protect the shallow roots and prevent drying out. Dense clusters of white bells cover the plant in early spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in acidic, well-drained soil in part shade.

directions: Plant in cool, acidic, humus-rich soil in light shade and mulch over the roots. Grown for its heavy, early flush of clean white bloom. Dense sprays of white flowers open in early spring above bronzy new growth.

planting.note: Plant in spring in damp, peaty, strongly acidic ground at a pond edge or bog margin.

directions: Sow seed thinly on the surface of an acidic peat mix and mist to keep it damp until sprouts appear. Give this low, suckering shrub constantly moist, boggy soil and it will slowly form a spreading colony. Whorls of deep rose-pink flowers appear below the new growth in early summer; note that the foliage is toxic to livestock.

planting.note: Plant in spring into saturated, acidic peat in full to part sun at a bog or water edge.

directions: Start seed on wet sphagnum peat under light and keep it perpetually damp, as this species is a true bog plant. Give it cold winters, constant moisture, and lean acidic soil rather than fertilizer. Small rose-pink cups open early in the season, well ahead of most laurels.

planting.note: Plant in spring in cold, wet mountain-meadow or bog soil that stays acidic.

directions: Surface-sow onto damp acidic peat and keep it bright and moist until the fine seedlings establish. This tiny western mat needs boggy, snowmelt-fed conditions and resents drying out or lime. Rosy-pink bowls open in late spring above the small rolled-edged leaves.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, acidic sandy peat at the edge of a pocosin or damp thicket.

directions: Sow the small seed on moist acidic peat under light and keep it damp through germination. This rare Carolinas native wants seasonally wet, sandy acidic soil and part shade. Nearly white, red-ringed flowers open in early summer as the wedge-shaped leaves expand.

planting.note: Plant in spring in damp, acidic peaty soil at a bog or seep edge.

directions: Start seed on the surface of wet acidic peat and hold it moist and bright. Closely allied to sheep laurel, this southeastern shrub wants steadily moist, lean acidic soil. Deep pink flower whorls open below the new leaves in late spring to early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in cool, acidic, well-drained soil; this named form is grown from cuttings.

directions: Set the rootball slightly high in loose acidic soil and mulch to keep the shallow roots cool and evenly moist. This classic selection carries bright red buds that open to soft pink stars in late spring. Shear off spent seed heads after flowering to steer energy into next year's buds.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in humus-rich acidic soil with sharp drainage.

directions: Plant high and mulch deeply so the fine roots stay cool and never waterlogged. Vivid red buds open to a bright pinkish-red, among the most intense colors in the group. Remove faded flower clusters to encourage strong bud set the following season.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in cool acidic soil; propagated by cuttings to stay true.

directions: Site in dappled shade with even moisture and set the crown slightly proud of the soil. Deep red buds open to rich pink flowers above glossy, wavy-edged foliage that resists leaf spot. Deadhead after bloom and prune lightly to shape once flowering ends.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in acidic, humus-rich, sharply drained soil.

directions: Plant high in loose acidic soil and keep a cool mulch over the roots year-round. White flowers are intricately marked with bands and streaks of purple-cinnamon inside each cup, giving a starburst effect. Snap off old seed clusters after flowering to keep the plant vigorous.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in a cool, acidic, well-drained spot suited to a compact form.

directions: This small-leaved dwarf fits foundation beds and containers; plant it high with a cool mulch. Light pink buds open to nearly white flowers on a dense, slow, compact mound. Trim spent blooms and it needs little other pruning to keep its neat shape.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in acidic, sharply drained soil in part shade.

directions: A compact grower for tight spots; set it slightly high and mulch to protect the shallow roots. Pale flowers carry a bold maroon-cinnamon band inside each cup on a small, dense plant. Remove old flower heads after bloom to keep it tidy and free-flowering.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in cool acidic soil suited to a small shrub.

directions: Plant this compact selection high with a cool organic mulch over the roots. Deep pink buds open to soft pink flowers on a dense, rounded, slow-growing plant. Clip away spent clusters after flowering and prune only lightly to shape.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in acidic, humus-rich soil with good drainage.

directions: Set the rootball high and mulch generously to keep the roots cool and moist. Rich pink buds open to bright pink flowers held in generous domed clusters. Shear off old seed heads after bloom to build strong buds for the next year.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in cool, acidic, sharply drained soil.

directions: Plant high in loose acidic soil and keep the shallow roots mulched and evenly damp. Very deep red buds open to pale pink flowers, giving a strong two-tone display in bud and bloom. Remove faded clusters after flowering to encourage next season's buds.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in acidic, well-drained, humus-rich ground.

directions: Site in part shade, plant high, and mulch to keep the fine roots cool and moist. Deep raspberry-red buds open to rich pink flowers above dark, burgundy-tinted stems. Deadhead spent clusters and prune lightly right after the bloom fades.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in cool acidic soil with sharp drainage.

directions: Plant slightly high and keep a cool mulch layer over the shallow root zone. Flowers are striped and streaked with pink candy-cane markings radiating from the center of each cup. Snap off old seed heads after bloom to keep the plant vigorous.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in acidic, well-drained, organic soil.

directions: Set the plant high and mulch to hold cool, even moisture around the roots. Each flower shows a broad purple-cinnamon band ringing a white edge and center, a striking target pattern. Remove spent clusters after flowering to steer growth into new buds.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in cool acidic soil with good drainage.

directions: Plant high in acidic humus-rich soil and keep the roots cool under mulch. Rich cinnamon-maroon banding on a light ground creates a spoked, pinwheel look in each cup. Deadhead after bloom and prune only lightly to shape.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in acidic, sharply drained, organic soil.

directions: Site in dappled light, plant high, and mulch to keep the shallow roots cool and moist. Deep red buds open to bright pink flowers for a fiery bud-to-bloom transition. Remove faded flower heads after the display to promote strong bud set.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in cool, acidic, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant high in loose acidic soil and mulch well to hold even moisture. Bright red buds open to pink flowers, lighting up the shrub at peak bloom. Shear away spent seed heads after flowering to keep the plant strong.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in acidic, humus-rich soil with sharp drainage.

directions: Plant high and mulch to keep the fine roots cool and evenly moist. Pure white flowers cover the plant, and the foliage shows good resistance to leaf spot. Deadhead spent clusters and give it good air movement to keep leaves clean.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in cool acidic soil that drains freely.

directions: Set the crown slightly high and keep a cool organic mulch over the roots. Pale flowers are dotted with a ring of ten cinnamon spots, one at each anther pocket. Remove old seed heads after bloom to keep the shrub vigorous.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in acidic, well-drained, humus-rich soil.

directions: Plant high in loose acidic soil and mulch to keep the roots cool and moist. Flowers are nearly solid deep burgundy-purple with only a thin light edge, among the darkest laurels. Deadhead after the display to encourage strong bud set the next year.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in cool, acidic, sharply drained soil.

directions: Plant high and keep the shallow roots mulched and evenly moist. Its petals stay separate rather than fused, so each flower looks like a five-pointed star instead of a cup. Remove faded clusters after bloom and prune only lightly to shape.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in acidic, humus-rich soil with good drainage.

directions: Plant high in loose acidic soil and mulch to keep the roots cool and moist. This selection carries unusual double flowers, a pink cup set within a cup for extra substance. Deadhead spent clusters after bloom to keep the plant vigorous.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in cool acidic soil that drains freely.

directions: Set the plant high and keep a cool mulch over the shallow roots. Deep maroon-banded flowers open from red buds for a bold two-tone effect. Snap off old seed heads after flowering to build next year's buds.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in acidic, well-drained, organic soil.

directions: Plant high in loose acidic soil and mulch to hold cool, even moisture. Cinnamon-maroon banding radiates across a light ground for a busy, patterned bloom. Remove spent clusters after flowering to keep the plant vigorous.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in cool acidic soil suited to a compact shrub.

directions: Plant this dwarf high and mulch to keep the shallow roots cool and moist. Deep red buds open to pink flowers on a small, dense, slow-growing mound good for containers. Trim away spent clusters and it needs little further pruning.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in acidic, humus-rich, sharply drained soil.

directions: Set the crown slightly high and keep the roots under a cool organic mulch. Flowers carry a rich cinnamon-red band on a lighter ground for a warm-toned display. Deadhead after bloom and prune lightly to maintain shape.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in cool acidic soil with good drainage.

directions: Plant high in loose acidic soil and mulch to hold even moisture around the roots. This selection is prized for unusually large white flowers, each cup wider than the typical form. Remove spent seed heads after bloom to keep the plant vigorous.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in acidic, sharply drained, organic soil.

directions: Plant high and keep a cool mulch over the shallow root system. Warm coral to salmon buds open to soft orange-pink flowers, an unusual color in the group. Deadhead spent clusters after flowering to encourage strong bud set.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in cool acidic soil that drains freely.

directions: Set the plant high and mulch to keep the fine roots cool and moist. Bright pink buds open to pale pink flowers with a soft blush at the throat. Remove old seed heads after flowering to keep the shrub vigorous.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in acidic, humus-rich soil with sharp drainage.

directions: Plant high in loose acidic soil and mulch to hold cool, even moisture. Crisp white flowers with narrow, pointed lobes give a clean, starry look in bloom. Deadhead after the display to steer energy into new buds.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, peaty, strongly acidic soil; grown from cuttings.

directions: Plant in damp, acidic, peaty ground and keep it steadily moist through the season. This selected form of sheep laurel bears deeper, richer red flowers than the wild type. Whorls of bloom open below the new shoots in early summer; keep foliage away from grazing animals as it is toxic.

planting.note: Plant in spring in dappled shade to bring out the pink and cream leaf variegation.

directions: Set the shrub in moist acidic soil where it receives filtered light, which deepens the cream, pink, and green leaf marbling. Keep roots cool with a thick organic mulch and avoid letting the soil dry out. Small white bell flowers appear in spring beneath the colorful arching foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring in part shade to enjoy the scarlet new growth.

directions: Plant this low, mounding form in cool moist acidic soil with the crown at grade. New spring growth emerges glossy scarlet, maturing to deep green and turning burgundy in winter cold. White urn-shaped flowers dangle from the stems in mid spring.

planting.note: Use as a low groundcovering shrub in spring plantings under shade.

directions: Plant this wide, low-growing selection in moist acidic woodland soil and let it spread as a groundcover. Mulch to conserve moisture and shade the shallow root zone. Fragrant white bell-shaped flowers line the arching branches in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shade so the white leaf margins stay crisp.

directions: Set in cool, evenly moist acidic soil where the white-edged foliage is shaded from hot sun. Keep it watered through dry spells and mulch the roots well. Small white urn-shaped flowers hang beneath the variegated leaves in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in part shade for a fine-textured woodland shrub.

directions: Plant in rich, moist, acidic soil in dappled shade with the crown set at grade. The slender leaves give a graceful fine texture and take on bronze tones in winter. White pendant flowers appear along the arching stems in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in part shade to showcase the twisted red-flushed leaves.

directions: Set this compact selection in moist, well-drained acidic soil in dappled shade. The curled, wavy foliage flushes red on new growth and in cold weather. White bell-shaped flowers appear along the stems in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a tidy low mound for shaded borders and containers.

directions: Plant this naturally dense, rounded form in cool, moist, acidic soil in partial shade. Glossy foliage takes on rich red and bronze tints through autumn and winter. Small white urn-shaped flowers hang from the branches in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a shaded rock garden or woodland edge.

directions: Plant this low Japanese species in gritty, moist, acidic soil in part shade. The glossy leaves turn deep red in winter, and its compact size suits rock gardens. Comparatively large white bell flowers hang in short clusters in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in part shade for intense ruby winter color.

directions: Set this compact selection in moist, gritty, acidic soil in dappled shade. New growth is glossy green, deepening to rich ruby red as temperatures drop in autumn and winter. White urn-shaped flowers appear in late spring to early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in consistently moist ground at a stream or bog edge.

directions: Plant this western species in wet, acidic, peaty soil where the roots never fully dry out. Unlike its arching relatives it holds upright clusters of flowers. Erect racemes of white urn-shaped blooms stand above the foliage in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist acidic soil in part shade.

directions: Plant this Japanese species in humus-rich, moist, acidic soil in dappled shade. The foliage colors well to bronze and red in cool autumn weather. One-sided racemes of greenish-white bell flowers open in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring on a moist wooded slope in acidic soil.

directions: Plant this Appalachian native in rich, moist, acidic woodland soil in partial shade. Its arching branches show good scarlet fall color as the leaves drop. Fragrant white bell-shaped flowers arch in curved racemes in mid spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a tall screen in moist shade in warm regions.

directions: Plant this vigorous southeastern native in moist, acidic soil in partial to full shade where it forms a tall, fountain-shaped screen. Glossy new growth emerges bronze-red and the crushed foliage has a distinctive scent. Fragrant white urn-shaped flowers hang along the arching stems in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a compact shade shrub for smaller gardens.

directions: Plant this dwarf, densely branched selection in moist, acidic soil in partial to full shade. It keeps the glossy foliage and bronze new growth of the species in a fraction of the size. Small fragrant white bell flowers dangle from the stems in spring.

planting.note: Set out dormant plants in early spring or in autumn while the soil is still workable.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil at the same depth the shrub grew in its container, and water deeply through the first summer to settle the roots. Prune out crossing branches right after the flowers fade, since blooms form on old wood. Watch for scarlet cup-shaped flowers along the bare stems in very early spring, often before the leaves unfurl.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring where its size and spines suit a hedge or barrier.

directions: Set this tall, spiny quince in full sun with room to spread, since it can reach the size of a small tree. Thin the oldest stems at the base every few years to renew the shrub and improve flowering. Expect pale pink to white blossoms in spring, followed by large, hard, fragrant fruits.

planting.note: Plant dormant nursery stock in early spring or autumn for a mid-height flowering shrub.

directions: This cross of Japanese and common quince wants full sun and tolerates a wide range of soils once established. Prune lightly just after bloom to shape the arching, spreading branches. Enjoy a heavy flush of showy flowers on bare wood in early spring.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; prized for mixed pink, white and red flowers on one plant.

directions: Site in full sun and well-drained soil, then leave established plants largely alone except for renewal pruning after bloom. The variety is famous for carrying pink, white, and red flowers together on the same branches, so avoid heavy shearing that removes flowering wood. Blooms open in early spring before or with the new leaves.

planting.note: Plant dormant in spring or fall; a pure-white-flowered form of common quince.

directions: Grow in full sun to light shade with average, well-drained soil for the cleanest white bloom. Thin the oldest canes after flowering to keep the interior open and encourage fresh growth. Clusters of snowy white flowers appear on bare stems in early spring.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; blossoms are white flushed with soft pink.

directions: Plant in full sun and well-drained soil, watering regularly the first season to establish. Prune after flowering and remove suckers to control the shrub's spread. The delicate pink-and-white flowers resemble apple blossom and open on bare wood in early spring.

planting.note: Plant dormant in spring; grown for its double creamy-white flowers.

directions: Set in full sun with well-drained soil and give it room for its spreading habit. Prune lightly after bloom to maintain shape without sacrificing next year's flowers. Enjoy fully double, creamy-white blossoms in early to mid spring.

planting.note: Plant dormant in spring; valued for twisted stems and white-pink blooms.

directions: Grow in full sun so the contorted, zig-zag branches develop their character. Prune minimally, removing only dead or crossing wood after flowering, to preserve the sculptural stems. White to pale pink flowers stud the twisted branches in early spring.

planting.note: Plant dormant in spring; large deep-red flowers on a low, spreading shrub.

directions: Plant in full sun and well-drained soil for the richest flower color. Thin old stems after bloom to renew the low, wide-spreading framework. Large crimson-red flowers open along the bare branches in early spring.

planting.note: Plant dormant in spring; distinctive greenish-yellow double flowers.

directions: Choose a full-sun position with average, well-drained soil for this collector's quince. Prune only lightly after flowering to keep the unusual blooms coming on older wood. Look for semi-double flowers in a soft yellow-green shade in early spring.

planting.note: Plant dormant in spring; thornless, fruitless double-orange selection.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained soil; the thornless stems make it easy to handle. It rarely sets fruit, so simply prune after flowering to maintain its upright shape. Large, fully double orange flowers resembling camellias cover the branches in early spring.

planting.note: Plant dormant in spring; thornless double-red selection with no fruit.

directions: Site in full sun for the heaviest flowering and give it well-drained soil. The spineless branches and lack of fruit make cleanup simple; just prune to shape after bloom. Dense, double scarlet flowers smother the bare stems in early spring.

planting.note: Plant dormant in spring; thornless double-pink selection with no fruit.

directions: Grow in full sun to light shade in average, well-drained soil. Because it is thornless and fruitless, pruning is limited to light shaping right after flowers fade. Ruffled, double coral-pink blossoms appear on leafless branches in early spring.

planting.note: Plant dormant in spring or fall; low, spreading shrub good for banks and hedges.

directions: Plant in full sun and well-drained soil; its low, wide habit suits mass plantings and slopes. Renew the shrub by cutting a few old stems to the ground after flowering. Deep crimson-red petals set off bright golden-yellow anthers in early spring.

planting.note: Plant dormant in spring; rose-pink flowers on a spreading shrub.

directions: Give it full sun and well-drained soil, watering through the first summer to establish. Thin crowded stems after bloom to keep good air flow and shape. Rich rose-pink flowers open early, often among the first shrubs to bloom in spring.

planting.note: Plant dormant in spring; large scarlet flowers on a spreading shrub.

directions: Plant in full sun with average, well-drained soil for the strongest bloom. Prune after flowering, removing older wood to encourage vigorous new shoots. Large, glowing scarlet-red flowers, sometimes semi-double, appear in early spring.

planting.note: Plant dormant in spring; a white-flowered sport of 'Texas Scarlet'.

directions: Set in full sun and well-drained soil; its compact, mounded form works well at the front of a border. Prune lightly after bloom to keep the low habit dense. Pure white flowers cover the arching stems in early spring.

planting.note: Plant dormant in spring; low, mounded shrub with bright red flowers.

directions: Grow in full sun for the best flowering; it tolerates heat and a range of soils once established. Its low, compact form needs only light pruning after bloom. Abundant tomato-red flowers stud the bare branches in very early spring.

planting.note: Plant dormant in spring; double apricot-pink flowers on a nearly thornless shrub.

directions: Plant in full sun and well-drained soil; the nearly spineless stems make this a garden-friendly choice. Prune after flowering to shape and to remove any dead wood. Fully double, peachy apricot-pink flowers open in early to mid spring.

planting.note: Plant dormant in spring; free-flowering orange-scarlet selection.

directions: Plant in full sun with well-drained soil for the heaviest flowering display. Thin some of the oldest stems after bloom to keep the shrub vigorous. Bright orange-scarlet flowers appear over a long stretch of early spring.

planting.note: Plant dormant in spring; low spreading shrub with blood-red flowers.

directions: Grow in full sun and average, well-drained soil; the wide, low habit suits hedging and slopes. Prune after flowering to control spread and renew growth. Deep signal-red flowers cover the branches in early spring.

planting.note: Plant dormant in spring; deep crimson flowers on a low, spreading plant.

directions: Site in full sun with well-drained soil and allow space for the spreading form. Remove a portion of old stems after bloom to keep the shrub open and floriferous. Rich blood-red flowers open along the bare stems in early spring.

planting.note: Plant dormant in spring; vigorous shrub with orange-red flowers.

directions: Plant in full sun and well-drained soil, watering well until established. Prune after flowering to guide the vigorous, spreading branches. Bright orange-red flowers appear on the leafless stems in early spring.

planting.note: Plant dormant in spring; free-flowering orange-red selection.

directions: Grow in full sun for the fullest flowering and give it average, well-drained soil. Thin old wood after bloom to encourage strong new shoots. Masses of orange-red flowers cover the branches in early spring.

planting.note: Plant dormant in spring; double apricot flowers on a compact shrub.

directions: Plant in full sun and well-drained soil; the compact habit fits smaller gardens. Prune lightly after flowering to keep it neat and floriferous. Soft double apricot-peach blooms open in early to mid spring.

planting.note: Plant dormant in spring; a dwarf, low-mounding form for the front of a bed.

directions: Site in full sun with well-drained soil; the dwarf, spreading habit makes good low groundcover. Shear lightly after flowering to keep it dense and compact. Orange-red flowers open in early spring, sometimes followed by small yellow fruits.

planting.note: Plant dormant in spring; grown in the Baltic region for its aromatic edible fruit.

directions: Plant in full sun and well-drained soil, and grow two or more plants nearby to improve fruit set. Prune after flowering to keep the shrub open and productive. Orange-red spring flowers give way to fragrant, lemon-like fruits used for juice and preserves in autumn.

planting.note: Plant this clonal selection in spring for its deeper pink bloom color.

directions: Give this selection full sun and room to arch, planting so the root flare sits at grade. It carries a richer pink flower than the species and blooms most heavily on wood from the previous year, so thin out old stems only after the spring show. Look for the flush of pink trumpet flowers in late May.

planting.note: Site the golden foliage where morning sun brings out its warm tones.

directions: Plant this gold-leaved form where it gets good light to keep the foliage bright chartreuse rather than fading to green. Water steadily the first couple of seasons and mulch the roots to hold moisture. Pink flowers open in late spring against the golden leaves, which shift toward orange and red in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring for reliably rose-pink flowers.

directions: Set this pink-flowered selection in full sun with the crown at soil level, allowing space for its graceful arching habit. It is tough and drought-tolerant once settled, needing only a light renewal pruning of old canes after bloom. The rose-pink bells arrive in late spring on year-old wood.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this suckering native fills in banks and dry shade well.

directions: Plant this low native shrub with the crown at grade in average or even poor, dry soil, where it slowly spreads by suckers to hold slopes. It tolerates sun or partial shade and needs little care once rooted. Small yellow tubular flowers open through summer and often blush reddish as they age.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a tough groundcovering shrub for banks and rough ground.

directions: Set plants at grade in well-drained soil; this adaptable native takes clay, drought, and part shade in stride and colonizes to cover ground. Cut it back hard in late winter if you want a tidier, denser mound. Clusters of sulfur-yellow flowers appear in early summer above bronze-tinted new growth.

planting.note: Plant in spring; valued for hairy foliage and summer flowers on tough sites.

directions: Plant at grade in average soil in sun or light shade, where this southeastern native tolerates heat and dry spells. It suckers modestly to form a broad mound and responds well to a hard late-winter cutback. Yellow flowers cover the shrub through summer, drawing bees and butterflies.

planting.note: Plant this vigorous hybrid in spring for glossy foliage and summer bloom.

directions: Plant this cross between the native bush honeysuckles at grade in well-drained soil, where its glossy leaves often carry a reddish cast. It thrives in sun or shade and needs almost no attention once established. Bright yellow flowers appear over a long summer stretch.

planting.note: Plant in spring; grown mainly for its orange new growth and fall color.

directions: Set this compact selection at grade in average soil; it takes sun or shade and shrugs off drought once rooted. New leaves emerge orange, and the foliage flames to orange-red in autumn. Small yellow flowers appear in summer, but the leaf color is the main draw.

planting.note: Plant in spring for deep red-tinted foliage that intensifies in fall.

directions: Plant this mounding shrub at grade in ordinary, well-drained soil in sun or part shade. The foliage carries red tones through the season and deepens to burgundy-red in cool fall weather. Yellow summer flowers add a modest accent above the colorful leaves.

planting.note: Plant in spring; prized for very dark, near-black purple foliage.

directions: Plant this dark-leaved selection at grade in average soil, giving it some sun to bring out the deepest purple-black tones. It is tough and low-maintenance, tolerating heat, drought, and part shade. Yellow flowers in summer contrast sharply against the near-black foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring for bright white-and-green variegated leaves.

directions: Plant this variegated form at grade in a spot with part shade to keep the white leaf margins from scorching. It brightens shady corners and spreads gently by suckers to fill in. Remove any all-green shoots that appear so the crisp white-and-green variegation stays true; small yellow flowers open in summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring for glowing dark burgundy foliage all season.

directions: Set this dark-leaved selection at grade in well-drained soil, giving it decent light to hold its rich burgundy color. It forms a tidy mound and needs little beyond an optional hard cutback in late winter. Yellow flowers in summer light up against the deep purple foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring for dark bronze-black foliage on a compact mound.

directions: Plant at grade in ordinary soil in sun or part shade, where this selection shows off dark bronze to blackish new growth. It is heat- and drought-tolerant and responds well to a hard renewal pruning in late winter. Yellow flowers appear through summer against the dark leaves.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a floriferous selection that draws pollinators.

directions: Plant this free-flowering selection at grade in well-drained soil in sun or part shade. It is adaptable and drought-tolerant, spreading slowly to form a broad mound on tough sites. Abundant yellow flowers over the summer make it a magnet for bees and butterflies.

planting.note: Set out rooted divisions or cuttings in early spring while the shrub is dormant.

directions: Plant in humus-rich, well-drained soil with the crown at the original nursery depth, ideally in dappled or afternoon shade to keep the flowers from bleaching. Prune out the oldest arching green canes right after bloom to renew the thicket and control suckering. Single buttercup-yellow flowers open along the stems in mid-spring, often with a light rebloom later in the season.

planting.note: Plant dormant divisions in spring where the tall canes have room to arch.

directions: Give this vigorous form room, as its bright green canes can reach eight to ten feet and spread by suckers. Thin and shorten the oldest stems just after flowering to keep the clump open and encourage fresh growth. The pompom-like double golden-orange flowers cover the branches in spring and hold well for several weeks.

planting.note: Set out in spring in a lightly shaded spot that protects the pale foliage from scorch.

directions: Plant this compact selection in dappled shade so the cream-margined gray-green leaves keep their crisp variegation. Watch for and prune out any all-green shoots that revert, since they grow more vigorously and can overtake the variegated growth. Single soft-yellow flowers appear in mid-spring against the delicate foliage.

planting.note: Plant dormant rooted stems in early spring in moist, well-drained ground.

directions: Site in partial shade in fertile soil and mulch to keep the roots cool and evenly moist. Renew the shrub by cutting the oldest canes to the ground after flowering. This selection is prized for its unusually large single golden flowers, up to two inches across, that open in mid-spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a shaded border where the pale blooms show well.

directions: Grow in light shade in humus-enriched soil, keeping it evenly moist through the growing season. Thin the arching green stems after bloom to maintain a graceful, open habit. The single flowers open a soft creamy pale-yellow rather than the usual gold, a subtle change that reads well in shaded plantings in mid-spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring where the striped winter stems can be enjoyed up close.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil in partial shade and leave some stems unpruned to display the winter bark. This form is grown for its green canes marked with yellow stripes, most striking after the leaves drop. Single yellow flowers appear along the stems in mid-spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; this tough shrub tolerates a wide range of soils and light.

directions: Set out in almost any soil from full sun to fairly deep shade, as this adaptable shrub tolerates urban conditions and drought once established. Prune after flowering to shape the arching branches, and note that the shiny black fruits are considered mildly toxic if eaten. Four-petaled white flowers open in late spring, followed by clusters of glossy jet-black bead-like fruits that persist into winter.

planting.note: Set dormant or containerized shrubs out in early spring once the ground can be worked.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic soil with the root flare level with the surface, and water deeply through the first season. The pearl-like buds open to clusters of white five-petaled flowers along the branches in mid to late spring. Prune lightly right after bloom to shape, since flowers form on the previous year's wood.

planting.note: Plant this compact hybrid in spring while the shrub is still dormant or freshly leafed out.

directions: Give this arching, mounded shrub full sun and room to weep, planting at the same depth it grew in the nursery pot. In spring the branches bend under a heavy show of pure white blooms that open from round pearl buds. Thin out a few oldest stems at the base after flowering to keep growth youthful and floriferous.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in a sunny, open spot with good drainage.

directions: Site this upright species where its pinkish new growth and white spring flowers can be appreciated up close. Plant in average, well-drained soil and avoid heavy, wet ground. The white racemes appear in mid-spring on year-old wood, so prune only after the bloom fades.

planting.note: Plant dormant shrubs in early spring in full sun.

directions: This vigorous variety carries some of the largest flowers in the genus, so give it space to reach full size. Plant in well-drained soil and water through dry spells until it is established. Expect a strong flush of large white blooms in mid-spring, followed by clean deciduous foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this species tolerates lean, alkaline soils better than most.

directions: One of the taller and more upright pearlbushes, this species is a good choice for tough, dry, or chalky sites. Plant in full sun with sharp drainage and it will need little supplemental water once rooted in. White flower clusters open in mid-spring on the previous season's growth.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in a sunny border with well-drained soil.

directions: Recognizable by its toothed leaf margins, this species makes a rounded, medium shrub for the mixed border. Plant at nursery depth in fertile, well-drained soil and mulch to conserve moisture. Large white flowers with notched petals open in spring; prune only to shape after bloom.

planting.note: Plant this named selection in spring while dormant or newly leafed.

directions: This selection is prized for an especially heavy, uniform bloom of large white flowers in spring. Plant in full sun and well-drained soil, keeping it evenly moist the first year. Remove a few of the oldest canes at ground level after flowering to encourage fresh, vigorous shoots.

planting.note: Plant this compact form in spring in a sunny, well-drained spot.

directions: A tidier, more compact pearlbush suited to smaller gardens, blooming freely on arching stems. Set the plant at the depth it grew in its container and water deeply until established. Masses of round white buds open to bright flowers in mid to late spring; shear lightly afterward to keep it dense.

planting.note: Plant in spring for the strongest establishment before summer heat.

directions: Named for its dense white spring display, this cultivar forms a rounded, mid-sized shrub. Plant in full sun and average, well-drained soil, mulching to keep roots cool. Enjoy the flurry of white flowers in spring and prune right after bloom, since buds set on old wood.

planting.note: Plant this improved selection in spring in full sun.

directions: Bred for an exceptionally full, rounded habit and heavy bloom, this shrub needs no staking or heavy pruning. Plant at nursery depth in well-drained soil and water through the first dry season. Round pearl buds cover the plant before opening to pure white flowers in spring.

planting.note: Plant this dwarf, rebloom-prone form in spring in a sunny bed.

directions: A dwarf pearlbush that flowers heavily in spring and often surprises with scattered rebloom later in the season. Plant in full sun and well-drained soil, keeping it watered while young. Its small size suits containers and the front of the border; trim lightly after the main flush to tidy it.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun with good drainage.

directions: Selected for large, luminous white blossoms carried on a well-branched, rounded shrub. Plant at the depth it grew in the pot and water deeply until roots spread. The big spring flowers stand out against fresh green foliage; prune only after they finish to protect next year's buds.

planting.note: Plant this garden hybrid in spring in full sun and well-drained soil.

directions: This cross combines free flowering with good hardiness and an arching, mounded shape. Plant in fertile, well-drained soil and water regularly during the establishment year. A generous spring show of white blooms covers the branches; renew the shrub by cutting a few old stems to the ground after bloom.

planting.note: Plant in spring into moist, acidic, organic soil in dappled light.

directions: Dig a wide hole in acidic, well-drained soil and keep the root zone mulched and evenly moist through summer. Prune only lightly after flowering to preserve the natural layered branching. Enjoy the deep red-and-cream bell clusters in late spring and brilliant red fall color.

planting.note: Best planted in spring in a sheltered, part-shade woodland spot.

directions: Site this cultivar in cool acidic soil enriched with peat or leaf mold and water regularly until well established. Its abundant deep-pink lantern flowers show best against a dark backdrop or evergreen neighbors. Bloom peaks in late spring, with rich crimson foliage following in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring into acidic, moisture-retentive soil in light shade.

directions: Give this shrub organic, acidic soil and steady moisture, avoiding hot dry sites that scorch the leaves. The deeply colored red bells are held in nodding clusters in late spring. Autumn brings intense wine-red foliage before leaf drop.

planting.note: Ideal for small gardens or containers; plant in spring in acidic mix.

directions: This compact selection suits a large container or the front of a woodland border in acidic, free-draining soil. Keep the shallow roots cool with mulch and never let the plant dry out completely. Small cream bells appear in late spring and the tidy mound turns fiery orange-red in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in cool, acidic soil with afternoon shade.

directions: Grow this white-flowered form in the same acidic, humus-rich conditions preferred by azaleas and rhododendrons. Its pure creamy-white bells lack the red veining of the species and glow in shaded corners. Flowers open in late spring and the leaves color richly in autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring into acidic woodland soil in part shade.

directions: Set this deeply colored form in moist, acidic soil rich in organic matter and keep roots shaded and mulched. It is prized for bells so heavily suffused with dark red that they appear almost solid crimson. Bloom comes in late spring ahead of vivid autumn foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring in acidic, well-drained soil in dappled shade.

directions: This variety carries reddish bells and shares the species' need for lime-free, humus-rich soil. Mulch annually with leaf mold and protect from drying winds. Flowers hang in dainty clusters in late spring, giving way to strong red fall color.

planting.note: Plant in spring into cool, acidic, organic soil in part shade.

directions: Grow in moist but well-drained acidic soil amended with plenty of leaf mold and shelter from midday sun. The fringed, nodding white bells hang in graceful racemes in late spring. Foliage turns clear red and orange before dropping in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in acidic woodland soil with light overhead shade.

directions: This red-flowered form wants the same cool, moisture-retentive acidic soil as the species and resents drought. Its fringed, deep-red nodding bells appear in dense racemes in late spring. Mulch yearly and expect glowing crimson autumn foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring in acidic, humus-rich soil in sun to part shade.

directions: This compact, twiggy species prefers cool acidic soil and even moisture and tolerates a little more sun than its relatives. Small urn-shaped white flowers open in early to mid spring before the leaves fully expand. It is valued as much for its dense habit and brilliant scarlet fall color as for its bloom.

planting.note: Plant in spring in acidic, well-drained soil in light shade.

directions: This selection forms a neat, rounded shrub well suited to smaller gardens and woodland edges. Provide acidic soil rich in organic matter and mulch to conserve moisture. White bell flowers appear in spring and the tidy foliage turns fiery red in autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in sheltered, acidic woodland soil in part shade.

directions: This larger species eventually forms a small tree in cool, moist, acidic soil and appreciates shelter from strong wind. Its pendulous bells are yellow to pink and prettily veined with red, opening in late spring. Give it room to develop its tiered branches and enjoy strong autumn tints.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, acidic soil in a sheltered part-shade site.

directions: Grow this vigorous Himalayan species in deep, humus-rich acidic soil with reliable summer moisture. It carries large clusters of bell flowers in shades of buff-yellow flushed red in late spring. Provide wind shelter and expect fine orange-red fall color on a broad, upright shrub.

planting.note: Plant in spring in acidic, well-drained woodland soil in part shade.

directions: This Chinese species bears comparatively large white, pendulous bells in mid to late spring before the leaves harden off. Give it lime-free, humus-rich soil and steady moisture in a spot protected from harsh sun. Mulch each spring and enjoy warm red and orange autumn color.

planting.note: In frost-free regions plant in spring; elsewhere grow in a cool container.

directions: This tender, evergreen species from southern China needs frost-free conditions, acidic soil, and protection from hot afternoon sun. Clusters of rosy-pink urn-shaped bells open in winter, a traditional Lunar New Year cut flower. Keep it evenly moist and shelter it in a cool greenhouse where winters are cold.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sheltered spot protected from cold wind.

directions: Give it sharply drained soil and a position with morning sun and afternoon shade to protect the leaves. The narrow cream leaf margins hold color best in bright but indirect light. Fragrant rosy-purple flower clusters appear from late winter and perfume the whole garden.

planting.note: Set out in spring in rich, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant slightly high in loose, organic soil and mulch lightly to keep roots cool. This selection carries broad golden-yellow leaf centers with green margins, brightening shaded corners. Deep pink buds open to fragrant pale flowers in late winter.

planting.note: Plant dormant stock in early spring or sow cleaned seed in fall for cold stratification.

directions: Site in cool, moist but well-drained loam and disturb the roots as little as possible. Seed needs a long cold period and often takes months to sprout, so most gardeners buy young plants. Rosy-purple flowers wreath the bare stems in very early spring, followed by bright red berries that are poisonous.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in cool, humus-rich soil.

directions: Grow in moisture-retentive but freely draining soil and avoid transplanting once settled. The white-flowered form produces amber-yellow berries instead of red ones. Fragrant pure-white blossoms clothe the leafless twigs in late winter to early spring.

planting.note: Set out low, spreading plants in spring on a gritty, well-drained bank.

directions: Plant this trailing evergreen in gritty, neutral-to-alkaline soil and top-dress the sprawling stems with grit to encourage rooting. Full sun and sharp drainage bring the best flowering. Masses of fragrant rose-pink flowers cover the mat in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in gritty, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Give this vigorous selection lean, sharply drained soil and mulch the spreading stems with grit. It bears larger, deeper rose-crimson flowers than the ordinary species. The intensely fragrant bloom flush peaks in late spring with scattered repeat later in summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a gritty, sunny raised bed or rock garden.

directions: Plant in humus-enriched but freely draining soil and keep the crown just above grade. This form is prized for its rich ruby-pink flowers and neat mounding habit. Fragrant spring bloom is often followed by a lighter second show in late summer.

planting.note: Set out container plants in spring in well-drained garden soil.

directions: Plant this semi-evergreen hybrid in average, well-drained soil and avoid heavy pruning, which it resents. It is more adaptable and easier than most daphnes. Clusters of sweetly scented pale-pink flowers open in late spring, often with a scattered repeat.

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile, well-drained soil in sun to light shade.

directions: Set the plant at its original depth in humus-rich, freely draining soil and mulch to keep the roots cool. Its leaves are neatly edged in creamy gold, giving season-long interest. Fragrant blush-pink flowers smother the rounded shrub in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile soil.

directions: Plant in average soil with good drainage and disturb the roots as little as possible when settling it in. This vigorous, upright form flowers freely and reliably. Pale-pink, powerfully fragrant blooms cover the bush in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring with afternoon shade to protect the pale foliage.

directions: Give it well-drained soil and light shade in hot climates, since the leaves can scorch in full sun. The foliage is nearly all creamy yellow with a thin green edge, the reverse of Carol Mackie. Fragrant pale-pink flowers appear in late spring against the luminous leaves.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sheltered position with rich, well-drained soil.

directions: Set this tall, upright daphne in fertile, moisture-retentive but well-drained soil sheltered from cold wind. It may be semi-evergreen to deciduous depending on the winter. Richly fragrant pink-and-white flower clusters open through late winter into early spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, sheltered border with rich soil.

directions: Plant in deep, humus-rich soil that drains freely, ideally near a doorway where the scent can be enjoyed. This is the most sought-after and evergreen form, grafted or cutting-grown for uniformity. Deep purple-pink buds open to intensely fragrant pale flowers from midwinter onward.

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile, sheltered ground.

directions: Give this vigorous upright form deep, well-drained soil enriched with leaf mold and shelter from drying wind. It carries larger, darker flowers than many bholua selections. Strongly scented rich pink blooms open through late winter.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, protected site.

directions: Plant in rich, freely draining soil sheltered from cold exposure and mulch to conserve moisture. This early-flowering selection begins its show sooner than most. Soft pale-pink, sweetly scented flowers open in early to midwinter.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shade under trees where little else thrives.

directions: Site this evergreen in shady, humus-rich soil where it tolerates dry root conditions once established. The glossy dark leaves give year-round structure in woodland plantings. Small green flowers appear in late winter, followed by black berries that are toxic.

planting.note: Plant in spring in gritty, well-drained soil in sun or light shade.

directions: Plant this compact evergreen in freely draining, humus-rich soil and shelter it from harsh wind. It is one of the hardier and more reliable daphnes for general gardens. Fragrant purple-pink buds open to white flowers in mid to late spring, sometimes followed by red fruit.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a raised bed or rock garden with sharp drainage.

directions: Set this slow, dense evergreen in gritty, freely draining soil and avoid wet winter conditions. Its compact rounded form suits troughs and small rock gardens. Fragrant rosy-purple buds open white in late spring, with scattered red berries after.

planting.note: Plant in spring in well-drained soil in full sun for best bloom.

directions: Plant this deciduous species in freely draining soil and give it a warm, sunny position. Root cuttings are the usual way to increase it, as it is tricky from stem cuttings. Lilac-blue, largely unscented flowers wreath the bare stems in mid spring before the leaves.

planting.note: Plant in spring in well-drained soil in sun to part shade.

directions: Plant this compact hybrid in average, freely draining soil and shear lightly after the first flush to keep it tidy. It is notable for blooming over an exceptionally long season. Fragrant white flowers from pink buds appear from late spring and continue on and off until frost.

planting.note: Plant in spring in well-drained soil in a bright position.

directions: Set in freely draining soil with good light, and clip lightly to maintain the mounded shape. Its gray-green leaves carry irregular creamy-white margins for added interest. Fragrant white flowers appear from late spring and repeat through the summer months.

planting.note: Plant in spring in rich, well-drained soil in sun to part shade.

directions: Plant in humus-rich, freely draining soil and site it where its powerful scent carries, as few daphnes are as strongly perfumed. This hybrid bears unusually large flowers over a long season. Pale pink-and-white blooms open from late winter into spring, with sporadic later flushes.

planting.note: Plant in spring in gritty, well-drained soil in a sheltered spot.

directions: Give this dense evergreen sharply drained soil and full sun, protecting it from cold wind. It forms a neat, rounded mound clothed in glossy dark leaves. Clusters of fragrant rose-purple flowers open in late spring, with occasional repeat in summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in freely draining soil in full sun.

directions: Plant this evergreen hybrid in gritty, well-drained soil and mulch lightly to keep the roots cool. It makes a tidy, spreading mound that flowers freely. Fragrant rosy-pink flowers open in late spring and often again in late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in sharply drained soil in full sun.

directions: Set in gritty, freely draining soil and give it a warm, sunny position for the best flowering. This compact selection has a low, mounded habit ideal for the front of a border. Fragrant deep-pink flowers appear in late spring with a lighter repeat later.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a trough or rock garden with very sharp drainage.

directions: Grow this tiny alpine evergreen in a gritty, lean mix with excellent drainage, ideally raised above winter wet. It forms a slow, dense cushion perfect for troughs and crevices. Clusters of fragrant rose-pink flowers cover the mat in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a tufa crevice or trough with extremely sharp drainage.

directions: This exacting alpine is usually grafted and needs a gritty, freely draining crevice that stays dry in winter. It grows extremely slowly into a congested, woody cushion. Comparatively large, fragrant deep-pink flowers smother the plant in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a trough or crevice with very sharp drainage.

directions: Grow this small Greek alpine in a gritty, lean mix in full sun with protection from winter wet. It forms a compact mound of tiny blue-green leaves. Fragrant white flowers, pink-flushed on the reverse, open in late spring and sometimes again in autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in gritty, well-drained soil in a sunny rock garden.

directions: Set this deciduous alpine in sharply drained, alkaline-leaning soil and give it full sun. It is one of the easier true alpine daphnes from seed. Clusters of fragrant white flowers open in late spring, followed by orange-red berries.

planting.note: Plant in spring in humus-rich soil in shade or part shade.

directions: Plant this shade-tolerant evergreen in moist, humus-rich but well-drained woodland soil. Its glossy leaves and spreading habit make good ground cover under trees. Spidery, night-scented pale-green flowers open in mid spring, followed by black berries.
